title: Australia more favourable for private debt: Metrics
full_text: Other Editions 05 May 2026 Business Australia more favourable for private debt: Metrics The Australian David Ross 4 May 2026 499 words English AUSTLN 13 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. A leading figure in Australia’s private credit market has warned comparisons with US private lenders miss the point, and the domestic market is structurally more favourable to investors. Metrics Credit Partners managing director Andrew Lockhart said while American lenders were famously exposed to software and technology loans, Australian lenders are tilted toward property loans, with the benefit of tighter controls around borrowing. “The pressure in parts of the US private credit market has been building for some time. Years of aggressive lending and structures designed for deal velocity rather than lender protection have left some managers poorly positioned as borrowing costs have risen and economic conditions have tightened,” Mr Lockhart writes in The Australian. “The US’s concentration in software and software as a service businesses, now facing genuine structural headwinds from artificial intelligence disruption, has compounded those pressures.” Mr Lockhart said enforcing on US lending is problematic, whereas Australia has a clear framework around tackling troubled loans. “That matters when things go wrong, as they will in some part of any portfolio over a meaningful investment horizon,” he said. “Sector exposure matters too. While parts of the US and UK markets leaned heavily into software, Australian private credit has typically focused on commercial real estate, infrastructure-adjacent lending, logistics and transport, and operating businesses backed by tangible assets.” Metrics is one of Australia’s largest non-bank lenders, with more than $35bn in assets under management. Mr Lockhart took aim at JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon, who warned of cockroaches in the US private credit market last year and repeated his wariness this year. Mr Dimon memorably said “when you see one cockroach, there are probably more”, after the collapse of credit market darlings First Brands and Tricolor. But he agreed Mr Dimon’s point about underwriting standards in private credit “is worth taking seriously everywhere”. “Any manager, in any market, can loosen credit discipline in pursuit of deal flow. Whether they have is a matter of fact, not geography.” Mr Lockhart encouraged investors to ask harder questions. “What does this portfolio actually lend against? What first-ranking security is held? What covenants are in place? How concentrated is the fund in any single sector or structure? Has this manager managed a distressed loan through to resolution, and what was the outcome?” Shares in listed US manager Blue Owl have been trounced in a private credit market reckoning, down 52 per cent over 12 months. Metrics’ listed funds have also suffered sell-offs. The corporate regulator is examining how private credit lenders in Australia advertise their returns as well as what fees they charge to clients. Mr Lockhart said Australia still has space for a robust private credit sector, as regulations around the banking system forced larger institutions out. “That structural gap has not closed. Australian businesses still need capital. Infrastructure still needs funding. Housing still needs to be built.” COMMENTARY P17 The Australian-20260504-Australian-13-000500647915 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: Older Australians will see an increase in private health insurance premiums by over $1600 starting April next year due to the government's decision to eliminate higher rebates for those aged over 65. This change is expected to significantly impact the affordability of health cover for seniors. The move has raised concerns about the financial burden on older Australians amidst rising living costs.
publication:
Seniors to cop a $1600 health cover hike
Daily Telegraph05 May 2026279 wordsJulie Cross

Older Australians are facing private health insurance price hikes of more than $1600 from April next year, under the Albanese government’s plan to scrap higher rebates for over-65s.
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Private health insurance premiums for seniors to rise by over $1600
Government to scrap higher rebates for those over 65
Concerns about affordability for older Australians
relevance: This news is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights changes in health insurance policy that could affect risk assessments and financial planning for older clients.
